data.title: New Zealand Rate Decision On Tap For Wednesday
data.description:
HONG KONG (dpa-AFX) - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand will wrap up its monetary policy meeting on Wednesday and announce its decision on interest rates, highlighting a modest day for Asia-Pacific economic activity.

The RBNZ is widely expected to keep its Official Cash Rate (OCR) unchanged at 2.25 percent.

South Korea will release February figures for its current account; in January, the current account surplus was $13.26 billion.

Japan will see February figures for its current account; in January, the current account surplus was 942 billion yen.

Hong Kong will see March results for its private sector PMI from S&P Global; in February, the index score was 53.3.

data.title: Corn, soy barge bids edge higher
data.description:
Spot basis bids for corn and soybeans shipped by barge to U.S. Gulf Coast terminals crept higher on Tuesday as traders awaited developments in the Iran war and the release of U.S. crop data.

  • U.S. President Donald Trump warned Iran that "a whole civilization will die tonight" if Tehran refuses to reopen the critical Strait of Hormuz shipping lane by his evening deadline.

  • Soybean futures are down 1% since before the war began on February 28, while corn futures are nearly unchanged.

  • Farmers have largely stopped selling crops as they wait for higher prices and focus on the spring planting season, traders said.

  • Export demand for U.S. corn has been strong but could weaken because fresh supplies from Argentina and Brazil will increasingly become available on the global market, a trader said.

  • Brazil exported 983,029 metric tons of corn in March, up from 871,297 metric tons a year earlier, and 14.52 million metric tons of soybeans, down from 14.66 million metric tons a year ago, its government said.

  • The European Union as of April 5 imported a total of 13.58 million metric tons of corn for the 2025/26 season that began in July, down about 18% from a year earlier, the European Commission said.

  • The bloc's 2025/26 soybean imports were 9.74 million metric tons by April 5, down 9% from the same period a year earlier, data showed.

  • Traders will watch to see whether the U.S. Department of Agriculture adjusts U.S. export and carryout estimates in a monthly supply/demand report on Thursday.

  • U.S. soybean and corn futures retreated on reminders of ample supplies on Tuesday, brokers said.

  • CIF Gulf corn barges loaded in April were bid 1 cent higher at 86 cents over Chicago Board of Trade May (CK26) futures.

  • FOB corn offers for vessels loaded in April were flat at 98 cents over futures.

  • CIF Gulf soybean barges loaded in April were bid 2 cents higher at 77 cents over CBOT May (SK26) soybean futures.

  • FOB export premiums for April soybean vessels remained unchanged at about 92 cents over futures.

For displays of CIF basis, click on the codes in brackets:

U.S. CIF Gulf soybeans (GRYM)

U.S. CIF Gulf corn (GRYN)

U.S. CIF Gulf HRW wheat (GRYP)

data.title: Why ASML Stock Is Down Today
data.description:
ASML declined about 4% on Tuesday following the arrival of a draft version of the MATCH Act in the U.S. House.

The bill seeks to align export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment among U.S. allies, including the Netherlands. Representative Michael Baumgartner said the measure would prevent China from obtaining tools needed to advance its chip production, the draft showed.

Citi Research analysts said the move could hurt ASML and may carry unintended consequences. Restricting sales of immersion lithography tools to China would likely limit that country's ability to expand production lines, according to a Tuesday note from analysts Andrew Gardiner and Daniel Schafei.

China lacks its own lithography tool production capability, the note added. ASML derived about 29% of its revenue from China in 2025. U.S. equipment makers sourced roughly 30% to 40% from China last year. Citi expects possible retaliation from Beijing using its rare earth minerals position.

data.title: Amazon Retains 80% USPS Deliveries, Over 1 Billion Packages Annually
data.description:
Amazon.com has reached a renewed agreement with the US Postal Service that keeps a critical piece of its rural logistics network intact, while potentially providing a much-needed revenue anchor for the government agency. Under the deal, Amazon is expected to retain roughly 80% of its existing USPS delivery volume, equating to more than 1 billion packages annually, according to a person familiar with the matter. Management framed the agreement as an extension of a longstanding partnership, with spokesperson Terrence Clark noting it would allow both sides to continue supporting customers and communities, particularly in harder-to-reach regions.

For the USPS, retaining Amazon's volume could be significant as the agency works through ongoing financial strain and the risk of a potential cash shortfall as early as next year, as previously warned by Postmaster General David Steiner. The agency has been exploring multiple levers to stabilize its finances, including seeking a higher borrowing limit from Congress and opening access to its delivery infrastructure to private-sector partners, a process Amazon participated in earlier this year. While financial terms of the new agreement were not disclosed, keeping the majority of its largest customer's business may provide some near-term visibility as restructuring efforts continue.

From Amazon's perspective, the agreement fits into a broader push to accelerate delivery speeds in rural America, where logistics remain more fragmented and costly. The company previously outlined a $4 billion investment aimed at improving service levels in these areas, including recruiting local business owners as part-time parcel carriers. By the end of this year, Amazon expects to operate 200 rural delivery stations to support last-mile fulfillment, a buildout that, when combined with continued USPS collaboration, could strengthen its positioning as it competes for a rural market that it has previously characterized as potentially reaching $1 trillion.

data.title: The Iran conflict isn't panicking investors - yet. That's about to change.
data.description:
By Joseph Adinolfi

Credit spreads haven't blown out, the oil-futures curve isn't pointing to a lasting supply shock, and the S&P 500 has so far avoided a correction

Financial markets have so far held up reasonably well despite the disruption to the global energy trade caused by the Iran conflict. But that could soon change, according to several Wall Street professionals who spoke with MarketWatch.

U.S. stocks have been stuck in a steady grind lower since the start of the Iran conflict in late February. But many on Wall Street are surprised that markets haven't fallen even further.

That applies not just to stocks, but to bonds as well. Corporate credit spreads - one of the most closely watched market barometers of economic stress - have remained relatively tame. At the same time, market-based gauges of investors' long-term inflation expectations point only to a modest pickup in long-term price pressures.

Investors seem complacent about the long-term risks to markets and the economy from here, according to several professional investors who spoke with MarketWatch. Yet that could start to change in the coming weeks as energy supply shortages in Europe and Asia grow more acute. Even if a deal is reached that causes the conflict in Iran to dramatically de-escalate, there are doubts that oil prices will immediately revert lower.

"I wouldn't say we're overly enthusiastic about the market here, but given the headline risk and what could happen, it doesn't make sense to be overly defensive quite yet," said Warren Pies, co-founder and chief strategist at 3Fourteen Research, in an interview with MarketWatch on Tuesday. "I'm not ready to buy the dip right now. Hug your benchmark, is what I'd recommend."

Even crude-oil prices are signaling that the disruption to global energy markets caused by Iran's attacks on regional refining and production infrastructure - as well as its decision to choke off the flow of tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz - will be relatively short-lived. The chart below shows the futures curve for U.S.-traded crude prices.

Pies cautioned that the oil-futures curve shouldn't be interpreted as a forecast for where spot prices are expected to sit down the road. Instead, a more helpful framework is to interpret the curve as a signal to help traders and end users of oil manage their inventories.

Still, the wide gap that has opened up between contracts expiring in the coming months and those expiring further out suggests that a degree of complacency about the supply picture has likely seeped into the market, Pies said.

But as investors wait for President Trump to strike a deal to extricate the U.S. from the conflict in Iran while restoring the flow of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, some on Wall Street cautioned that time is quickly running out.

"There are a lot of folks talking about mid-April as something of an end date. If we don't see a resolution by mid-April, that will ultimately result in strains in production into the longer run," said Gina Martin Adams, chief market strategist at HB Wealth, during a Tuesday interview with MarketWatch.

Confidence or complacency?

Investors across Wall Street have been wondering why stocks in particular have held up so well since the start of the conflict. As of Tuesday's close, the S&P 500 SPX was trading about 5% below its January record high, FactSet data showed.

That question seemed particularly relevant on Tuesday. As the evening deadline for Trump's latest ultimatum to Iran approached, U.S. stocks struggled for direction. But, like much of the trading action since the conflict began, any declines have been relatively subdued. The S&P 500 even ended 0.1% higher on the day after a choppy, headline-driven session.

"Markets have been remarkably calm given the Iran situation and the implications for global energy flows," said a team of macro strategists at Mizuho Securities, in commentary shared with MarketWatch via email on Tuesday.

"So what's going on? It's probably a mix of complacency and confidence. Investors aren't ignoring the risks, but they're clearly leaning on history," the Mizuho team noted. Previous oil price shocks that followed the start of the first Gulf War, the U.S. invasion of Iraq and Russia's invasion in Ukraine taught investors that energy markets can recalibrate more quickly than the headlines might suggest.

"Net result: Investors feel insulated, rightly or wrongly. That's why we're seeing muted reactions as we head toward tonight's deadline - not because the risks aren't real, but because markets are increasingly conditioned to look through them," the Mizuho team continued.

The fact that the so-called fundamentals appear to be holding up also has helped to serve as a buffer for markets. The strength of the underlying economy usually influences both credit markets and stocks.

Official data released for March have so far shown that the U.S. economy held up reasonably well during the opening weeks of the conflict. Far more jobs were created last month than economists had expected, according to Friday's update from the Labor Department. That said, it is still early, and investors will receive an update on inflation later this week that could spur some volatility if the numbers are stronger than expected.

Following Trump's "liberation day" tariff announcement last April, Wall Street analysts moved quickly to lower their earnings forecasts, while strategists scrambled to slash their year-end price targets for the S&P 500. But after markets swooned, Trump turned around and walked back some of the tariffs. That helped teach investors and Wall Street analysts a lesson they won't soon forget, said 3Fourteen's Pies.

This time around, analysts have been much more careful about embracing a darker outlook. Over the past month, they have mostly maintained or raised their forecasts for how much money major publicly traded companies are expected to earn in profits, both during the first quarter and for 2026 as a whole.

Optimistic guidance from corporate management has also played a role, as more companies have issued positive guidance for the first quarter than negative guidance, according to FactSet analyst John Butters.

But more recently, analysts' upgrades have mostly focused on two sectors: information technologym which is more insulated from an energy-driven price shock, and energy, which tends to benefit from higher oil and gas prices.

"Outside of the energy space, analysts are mostly sitting on their hands, waiting until the next earnings season to get a better picture of the impact," said Bob Elliott, a former executive at hedge fund Bridgewater Associates and co-founder of Unlimited Funds, during an interview with MarketWatch on Tuesday.

But as companies start to report results, analysts could start to lower their expectations for the balance of the year.

"You're not going to meaningfully adjust your estimates until you get guidance from the company," Elliott said. "But they might update their views after."

Stocks ended Tuesday largely unchanged, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite COMP eking out modest gains. The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA wasn't so lucky; it finished in the red, although it was down by less than 100 points.

U.S.-traded crude-oil prices (CL00) (CL.1) (CLK26), meanwhile, were still trading above $110 a barrel on Tuesday, based on the May contract for West Texas Intermediate crude.

-Joseph Adinolfi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Medicare Finalizes 2.48% Rate Increase, Lifting Health Insurer Outlook
data.description:
Health insurers are seeing a sharp reset in sentiment after US regulators finalized a higher Medicare Advantage payment update than initially proposed, a shift that could begin to stabilize earnings expectations across the group. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services confirmed a 2.48% rate increase for 2027, improving meaningfully from the near-flat proposal outlined in January that had weighed heavily on the sector. The policy continues to dictate how more than half a trillion dollars flows through private plans, with CMS indicating the change could result in over $13 billion in additional payments next year, offering some relief as insurers contend with elevated medical costs.

The market reaction was immediate and telling. UnitedHealth Group rose 6.6% in premarket trading, Humana climbed 11%, and CVS Health advanced 5.8%, suggesting investors may be recalibrating expectations after the earlier selloff tied to the January proposal. That earlier framework, which effectively held rates flat, had erased nearly $100 billion in market value across Medicare-focused insurers, as companies flagged the difficulty of absorbing higher utilization without corresponding reimbursement support. Into the final decision, expectations had centered on a modest improvement, with analysts pointing to a roughly 1 to 1.5 percentage point increase as potentially sufficient.

What stands out now is not just the higher rate, but the broader policy tone. While the increase itself is not outsized in isolation, it could allow insurers to rebuild margins if cost discipline and benefit adjustments continue. At the same time, CMS opted to defer certain changes to the risk adjustment model, a decision that may signal a more measured regulatory approach after industry pushback. Taken together, the combination of improved funding visibility and delayed structural changes could set up a more constructive earnings trajectory over the next couple of years, even as regulators continue trying to balance near-term program stability with longer-term sustainability.

data.title: Oil Prices Decline Ahead of Trump's Hormuz Deadline — WSJ
data.description:
By Ryan Dezember

Oil futures opened trading lower Tuesday evening in New York ahead of President Trump's 8 p.m. ET deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of the world's oil supply flows.

West Texas Intermediate futures for May delivery settled Tuesday afternoon at $112.95 a barrel, up 0.5% and about 69% higher than at the end of February. Though the benchmark domestic oil price pushed to its highest price in nearly four years, Tuesday's session produced the second smallest daily move since the U.S. and Israel began bombing Iran.

The relatively subdued move echoed the muted activity in other markets Tuesday, as traders and investors braced for outcomes ranging from capitulation from Trump, who in recent days has stepped up his battle cries, to an escalation in the fighting.

"A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again. I don't want that to happen, but it probably will," Trump wrote Tuesday morning on Truth Social.

As the deadline loomed, Pakistan's prime minister urged Trump to extend it for two weeks and called on Iran to cede to Trump's demand to allow ships through the strait.

"We continue to see the near-term conflict path clearly pointing to escalation, even if the White House announces another extension," RBC Capital Markets' head commodity strategist Helima Croft wrote in a note to clients Tuesday afternoon. "We think Iran will continue to prioritize keeping oil prices high and therefore only allow a small number of fee-paying ships to pass."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Super Micro's Board Investigates Alleged Smuggling of Nvidia Chips to China — Update
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Super Micro Computer said a group led by members of its board has launched an independent investigation related to the indictment of two former employees and a contractor over an alleged scheme to smuggle high-end Nvidia chips to China.

The server maker said Scott Angel, lead independent director of its board, and Tally Liu, who chairs the board's audit committee, are leading the investigation. Angel and Liu will report their findings to the other four independent members of the board, the company said.

The group has also enlisted the help of law firm Munger, Tolles & Olson, as well as AlixPartners as an independent consultant. The group plans to work with Super Micro's auditor BDO USA.

The independent directors have not set a timeline for the investigation. Super Micro said it would provide an update once the investigation is complete.

The investigation comes amid an alleged scheme to smuggle Nvidia chips to China, which involved billions of dollars of servers and dummy devices used to deceive an American inspector, according to a U.S. indictment.

Super Micro said it no longer has a relationship with the three individuals allegedly involved in the scheme. The company had previously placed its co-founder Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw, who was a senior vice president, on leave after learning of his alleged role in the scheme, after which Liaw resigned. Super Micro also terminated a second employee who was previously placed on leave and fired a contractor.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Samsung Rides AI Boom To Massive Profit Growth
data.description:
Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) is seeing a huge boost from the AI wave, and it is showing up clearly in its latest outlook.

The company expects Q1 operating profit to come in around 57.2 trillion won, up from about 6.7 trillion won a year ago. That is more than an eightfold jump and well above estimates near 40.6 trillion won. Revenue is also set to climb about 68% year over year to roughly 133 trillion won, putting it close to record levels.

A lot of this comes down to memory chips. Demand for high-bandwidth memory, which is used in AI accelerators and data center GPUs, has been strong, and Samsung is benefiting from better pricing as well. On top of that, the weaker Korean won, now near a 17 year low against the dollar, is giving an extra lift to earnings. this is another example of how much the AI cycle is reshaping the chip industry.

The next key update will come with the full earnings report later this month, which should give a clearer breakdown of where the growth is coming from.

data.title: Intel Stock Rises After Joining Musk's Terafab Project
data.description:
Intel rose about 2% on Tuesday morning after joining the Terafab semiconductor project alongside Elon Musk's SpaceX, xAI and Tesla , according to a company post on X.

The chipmaker said its ability to design, fabricate and package high-performance chips at scale could help Terafab reach its goal of producing one terawatt per year of compute for AI and robotics. Intel also hosted Musk at its facility over the weekend, the post showed.

SpaceX described the effort as an epic chip-building venture combining logic, memory and advanced packaging under one roof, according to a separate post on X. Musk unveiled plans for Terafab last month, with two advanced chip factories planned in Austin, Texas, to gain more control over semiconductor supply for AI and space computing.

Tesla shares slid about 2% on Tuesday. Wedbush analysts said the Terafab facility may represent a first step toward a potential merger between Tesla and SpaceX, possibly as soon as next year.

data.title: Arm Holdings Stock Sinks On Morgan Stanley Downgrade
data.description:
Arm Holdings fell about 6% on Tuesday after Morgan Stanley lowered its rating on the British chip designer to Equal-Weight from Overweight.

Analyst Lee Simpson said the company's move into chipmaking represents a structural shift in its business model, aligning with the rise of agentic AI. In an agentic computing environment, graphics processing units may handle generation and reasoning while central processing units coordinate and execute tasks, the note explained.

Simpson acknowledged that Arm's new AI-focused CPU design shows the chip is not becoming obsolete. He also praised the company's talent acquisition and strategic positioning. However, the commercial ramp could take time, and near-term risks appear to temper enthusiasm, according to the analyst.

Investor focus may return to Arm's in-line guidance against a challenging demand backdrop. End-market softness and DRAM supply constraints could slow near-term growth. Margins also appear in flux, with higher research and engineering costs expected before meaningful chip revenue materializes, the note said.

Simpson added that Arm's entry into silicon production may increase channel conflict. Competing with parts of its customer base could lead to pushback, a risk investors should not discount.
